title: Lesson 377
transcript: Welcome back for another one. In this section, we've been talking about the importance of compliance, something that is obviously very important to all organizations. Now, when you think about what compliance is, and we're going to explain it here, You know, this is something that organizations cannot do unless security is involved. It's a perfect example of how security today has to do much more than just focus on protecting data or protecting against IT risks. Because when you think about what compliance is, compliance is the ability of the organization to, first of all, understand and be able to meet all of the requirements related to things like laws, regulations, industry best standards, Privacy requirements and so on. So it's very important, depending on the organization itself and what it does to understand the laws, the regulations, the privacy requirements, the industry best standards and so on. that it has to, first of all, understand, and secondly, be able to comply to. And in most cases, laws and regulations that pertain to things like privacy and data protection require the perfect implementation of security controls to allow the organization not only to be able to meet the requirements of complying to laws, regulations, and privacy requirements, but to also prove it to its stakeholders. And by stakeholders, we mean obviously internal stakeholders. For example, the board of directors, management, the executive, the CEO, they need to know that we've got the proper security controls in place to be able to meet all of those requirements that are related to laws and regulations, including privacy as it says here. And not only that, but we have to be able to show on a regular basis that security has the right levels of security controls to be able to meet the requirements of compliance. You can't expect the security function to understand all of the laws and regulations and so on that apply to the organization. This is why a CEO, for example, that is ultimately accountable for compliance, requires functions that are led by people that are, for example, experts in the laws and regulations. And so it's not uncommon for organizations to have functions called legal and compliance and privacy. And these are functions in the organization that are going to be led by people that understand the specific laws and regulations that apply to the organization, depending on which areas of the world They're operating in and have entities in and have customers in and so on. This is really important, but then it's also very important for those people that are accountable for compliance, including the CEO, to prioritize and justify and mandate, for example, what security needs in order to ultimately allow the organization to meet its compliance requirements, including those that are focused on privacy. So the reason we're mentioning here security and privacy because an organization may have a whole bunch of laws that are focused on data protection, for example, but also meeting the requirements of privacy laws and regulations that tend to differ in all kinds of different areas around the world. A perfect example of these are things like GDPR that not only applies to Europe, for example, and citizens that obviously live in Europe, but it has far-reaching implications to other areas of the world as well. But the way that privacy has been evolving throughout the world is that these laws are becoming much more stringent. and therefore require the perfect implementation of security controls to be able to allow the organization to meet its requirements related to things like privacy. And so just as an example, anytime you see a list just like this one, It's not meant to be an all-inclusive list. It's just giving you examples of, in this case, certain things that would be part of this umbrella this really important corporate governance activity called compliance that ultimately the CEO and the board of directors are accountable for, but cannot be achieved without the direct involvement of the security function. How can you meet the requirements of laws and regulations and industry best standards and privacy requirements and some other laws related to things like data protection and so on, unless you've got the perfect security controls in place to protect all of that and what we call compliance. Now, trans-border data flow regulations, if you look at this, if you think about the words, tells you exactly what this is focused on. And so countries around the world will have laws that will prohibit, for example, organizations and companies that operate within that country's borders from being able to send out and even receive in information that can impact certain things like national security and individual privacy and even economic well-being. These laws that are focused on what can go across borders, for example. are there to really protect those three things, right? National security, individual privacy of the citizens that live within that country, but also economic wellbeing, which sometimes is referred to as economic sovereignty. That's what transporter data flow regulations that exist in every country are focused on. And import and export controls. Countries will have rules and regulations that, again, will dictate what companies can bring in or bring out as far as products are concerned and a lot of these relate to military products and I think Rob's going to talk more about specific ones like ITAR and EAR that would fit in this umbrella of import and export controls especially those related to things like encryption and military products, for example. So in this section, we've basically summarized this concept of compliance and how really it requires the perfect involvement of security because unless security does the right things related to security controls, the organization will not be able to meet its requirements related to compliance, to laws and regulations and privacy requirements and some of the other things that you see here.
